title: Te Whakatau Tapa Whenua: Geometry and Symmetry in Tukituki River Pā Sites / The Art of Land Division: Geometry and Symmetry in Tukituki River Pā Sites
subject: Mathematics
culturalContext: Students will explore the sophisticated geometric knowledge embedded in the pā sites along Te Awa o Tukituki, specifically focusing on the defensive earthworks and strategic layouts of Ngāti Kahungunu pā like Ōtātara and Pākōwhai. The lesson connects mathematical concepts of symmetry, angles, and geometric patterns to the ancestral engineering that protected our people for generations.
learningObjectives: Identify and measure angles of elevation and defensive sightlines in local pā site diagrams using protractors
Create scale drawings of pā earthwork layouts demonstrating understanding of geometric proportions and symmetry
Calculate perimeter and area measurements of pā defensive structures using appropriate units and formulas
Explain how geometric principles were applied in traditional pā construction for both practical and spiritual purposes

activities.title: He Tirohanga Whakamua: Pā Site Perspective Analysis
activities.description: Students examine aerial photographs and historical diagrams of local Ngāti Kahungunu pā sites, identifying geometric shapes and patterns. Using protractors, they measure angles of defensive walls and calculate optimal sightlines for protection.

activities.title: Te Hoahoa Whakatau: Scale Drawing Creation
activities.description: Working in pairs, students create accurate scale drawings of pā layouts, applying knowledge of ratios and proportions. They must include key features like palisades, terraces, and food storage pits, labeling measurements and scale factors.

activities.title: Te Whakamātau Mataara: Perimeter and Area Calculations
activities.description: Using their scale drawings, students calculate the perimeter of defensive walls and area of different pā sections. They compare these measurements to modern equivalents (e.g., 'the defensive wall was equivalent to the length of 15 classrooms').

activities.title: Te Hono ki te Whenua: Field Connection
activities.description: A visit to local pā sites (virtual or physical) where students verify their calculations, observe the actual geometry, and discuss with local kaumātua how mathematical knowledge was transmitted orally through generations.

activities.title: He Whakaaro Hou: Modern Applications
activities.description: Students design a contemporary community space using pā design principles, creating a presentation that explains the geometric concepts employed and how they honor traditional knowledge while serving modern needs.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu Iwi Incorporated pā site archives and maps
Local kaumātua Hone Smith (expert in traditional pā construction)
Hawke's Bay Cultural Trust virtual pā site tour resources
NZ Maths Geometry and Measurement achievement objectives documentation

tasks: Complete a geometric analysis report of a chosen pā site including angle measurements, scale calculations, and perimeter/area findings
Create an accurate scale drawing of a pā layout with mathematical annotations
Present a 3-minute explanation of how geometric knowledge was embedded in traditional pā design

nzcAlignment: Geometry and Measurement: Level 4 - Use scales, plans, and maps; Find perimeters and areas of rectangles and related shapes; Measure angles using degrees
Number and Algebra: Level 4 - Use multiplication and division to solve problems involving rates and ratios
Te Tiriti o Waitangi: Honoring Māori mathematical knowledge systems and traditions
Key Competencies: Using language, symbols and texts; Thinking; Participating and contributing
